attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old man was brought in by ambulance to the Emergency Department (ED), after having collapsed and been found unconscious at home by his neighbour. Through discussions with his neighbours, paramedics were able to ascertain that his medical history includes multiple bites from redback spiders while working in the garage in the last few days, and Type 2 diabetes mellitus. His medications include regular metformin. He has a 40-pack-year smoking history.

On examination, his blood pressure and pulse were unrecordable. His SPO2 was 92% and temperature 37.4°C. He was given IM adrenaline and IV fluids through an intraosseus line by the paramedics. Upon arrival to the ED, he was resuscitated using IV fluids and a continuous IV infusion of adrenaline, and transferred to the Intensive Care Unit (ICU) for ongoing management. On day four of his admission to ICU, the registrar notes on the ward round the findings shown in the photograph (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely explanation for the changes shown in the photograph?
attributes.explanation:
The photograph of this patient's hand demonstrates digital ischaemia, as demonstrated by the diffuse necrosis involving the distal phalanx, and entire nail bed. The most likely in this case is due to vasopressor usage, as this patient was likely being given adrenaline as part of treatment to correct his haemodynamic instability, and maintain his mean arterial pressure. The vasoconstriction mediated by the alpha-1 recoptor agonism of adrenaline can lead to ischaemic changes of the terminal aspects of fingers and toes. Ischaemia of this kind is usually bilateral and symmetrical.

While trauma (i.e. crush injuries) is a potential cause for necrosis of terminal aspects of phalanges as in this case, it is more likely that this patient developed this secondary to ischaemia, given his significant vascular risk factors.

A sub-ungual melanoma is unlikely to be the cause of this finding, as these usually present with melanonychia (i.e. a darkening of the nail plate, involving the entirety of the nail). While they can sometimes extend, creating discolouration of the surrounding skin (Hutchinson sign), the extent of the involvement of the finger here makes this unlikely to be the cause.

Meningococcal sepsis is unlikely to be the cause of this finding, given that the patient was afebrile, not photophobic, and there is no mention of a non-blanching petechial rash on examination.

While spider bites have been reported as an extremely rare potential cause of digitial necrosis, and this patient does have a history of being bitten by spiders, the patient would be much more likely to present with other signs of envenomation following a spider bite (e.g. significant coagulopathy, neurologic deficits).
learningPoints: High dose adrenaline is a potential cause of digitial ischaemic necrosis, particularly in those with pre-exisiting vascular risk factors
references: Daroca-Pérez R, Carrascosa MF. Digital necrosis: a potential risk of high-dose norepinephrine. Therapeutic advances in drug safety. 2017;8(8):259-61. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5518967/

Vetter RS. Myths about spider envenomations and necrotic skin lesions. The Lancet. 2004;364(9433):484-5. Available at: https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736(04)16824-4/abstract
